title: GUIDELINES FOR THE CONTROL OF TAENIA SAGINATA

sections.section: GUIDELINES FOR THE CONTROL OF TAENIA SAGINATA
sections.summary: Document title only — Guidelines for the control of Taenia saginata.
Heading only — indicates the Guidelines focus on control of Taenia saginata in meat of domestic cattle.
Document code only (CAC/GL 85-2014).

Defines bovine cysticercosis as infection of cattle striated muscle with Taenia saginata metacestodes, explains humans acquire taeniasis from eating raw/undercooked beef with live cysticerci, notes limited public health impact but substantial economic effects (inspection costs, carcass downgrading/condemnation, farm controls), highlights low sensitivity of routine post‑mortem inspection, and states the Guidelines adopt a risk management framework (preliminary activities, option selection, implementation, monitoring and review).

Primary objective: provide governments and industry with guidance on risk‑based measures to control T. saginata in meat of domestic cattle and to give a consistent technical basis for reviewing national/regional measures and judging equivalence for trade.

sections.section: ## 3. SCOPE AND USE OF THE GUIDELINES
sections.summary: These Guidelines, to be used with FAO/WHO/OIE Taeniasis guidance and the Code of Hygienic Practice for Meat, address control of cysticercosis in domestic cattle meat across the primary production‑to‑consumption chain and provide supplemental, flexible risk‑based guidance including reference to OIE diagnostic techniques for governments, industry and equivalence assessments.

Scope: addresses control of cysticercosis in meat of domestic cattle that may cause human taeniasis and applies across the primary production‑to‑consumption continuum, used with FAO/WHO/OIE Taeniasis Guidelines and CAC/RCP 58‑2005.

Use: provides specific, risk‑based guidance for selecting post‑harvest control measures for bovine cysticercosis, supplements general food hygiene and meat codes, references OIE diagnostic methods, and is intended to be flexibly applied by competent authorities and industry (including for equivalence judgements).

Defines 'domestic cattle' to include Bos (taurus, indicus, banteng, gayal, yaks) and additionally Bubalus and Bison species, and defines 'herd' as animals of one kind kept together under human control.

sections.section: 5. PRINCIPLES APPLYING TO CONTROL OF BOVINE CYSTICERCOSIS
sections.summary: Principles: meat hygiene programmes should incorporate food safety risk analysis, monitoring of slaughter and human surveillance data should inform hygiene requirements, and competent authorities should recognise equivalent alternative hygiene measures that achieve required safety and suitability outcomes and fair trade practices.

Preliminary risk management activities include developing national or regional risk profiles (building on generic Codex profiles) and evaluating epidemiological evidence to guide selection of appropriate, risk‑based control measures.

sections.section: 6.1 Identification of a food safety issue
sections.summary: Advises preliminary RM activities of developing a national/regional risk profile and evaluating epidemiological evidence to support a risk‑based approach relative to the local or trade situation.

Risk profiles collate scientific and epidemiological information (e.g., slaughter records, farm investigations, human surveillance and treatment data) to guide risk managers and industry in designing tailored food control systems; generic profiles are available from FAO/WHO repositories.

sections.section: 7. IDENTIFICATION, SELECTION AND IMPLEMENTATION OF RISK-BASED CONTROL MEASURES
sections.summary: Provides a suite of risk‑based control options: farm‑level measures per FAO/WHO/OIE guidance, post‑slaughter measures (meat inspection, confirmation of suspect cysts, alternative inspections, validated treatments, traceability, movement control), and explains that slaughter populations can be classified as low prevalence using surveillance or public health data so that risk modelling may justify reduced inspection intensity, while noting incursions can still occur from external sources.

Advises that farm‑level control measures should be selected and applied in conjunction with the FAO/WHO/OIE Taeniasis Guidelines and cover all steps of the primary production‑to‑consumption continuum.

Post‑slaughter options center on meat inspection (with histopathology confirmation of suspect cysts where practicable), recognition that routine inspection has low sensitivity, availability of alternative inspection procedures, validated meat treatments (heat, freezing, salting, irradiation), traceability between slaughterhouse and farm, and potential for competent authorities to impose movement controls and intensified surveillance.

Post‑mortem inspection: routine control is primarily meat inspection with histopathological confirmation of suspect cysts where practicable, laboratory tests must have known sensitivity/specificity, routine inspection sensitivity for T. saginata is very low (especially in light infections), and inspection range/intensity varies by country.

Alternative inspection procedures: when a suspect carcass or part is identified, additional inspection of that carcass, its parts and cohorts can increase detection sensitivity, with procedures and intensity varying by country.

Treatment of meat: validated temperature treatments (heating/freezing) are routine preventative controls and are used for suspect or confirmed carcasses and cohort carcasses, while salting and irradiation may be used if validated and approved by the competent authority.

Traceability: competent systems linking slaughterhouses to places of production should be in place so positive or suspect carcass information can trigger farm‑level control measures and notification of suspect cohorts for intensified inspection.
The competent authority may impose movement control requirements on herds when monitoring data indicate such risk-based measures are appropriate.

Section heading introducing selection of risk-based control measures, including criteria for regarding slaughter populations as low prevalence and options for adjusting post-mortem inspection intensity.

Slaughter populations can be classed as low prevalence when slaughterhouse data, public‑health data or other epidemiological information show absent/very rare infection, and in such cases risk modelling may justify derogations or reduced intensity of routine post‑mortem inspection (e.g. palpation/incision) which the competent authority should apply; intensified inspection of individual carcasses and related groups is recommended when suspect cysts are detected, and the text warns that incursions can occur despite historical data (including from contaminated feed or infected people).

Requires a robust monitoring system using slaughterhouse organoleptic post‑mortem inspection and laboratory reports (e.g. histopathology) to evaluate control performance, with trace‑back to farms when suspect cysts are found, application of on‑farm controls and intensified inspection as required, notification of intensified inspection results to the competent authority, and involvement of public health authorities.

Obliges communication of best practices for control of Taenia saginata in cattle to all production stakeholders and basic public‑health training on the parasite life cycle for persons involved in cattle production, and directs the competent authority to make monitoring/investigation information public and run education campaigns when there is a public health risk.
